sessions.title: Potential Energy
indicators: Define potential energy
Give some examples of potential energy
Calculate questions involving potential energy
sessions.content:
What is Energy?

Energy is the ability to do work. Modern civilization is possible because people have learned how to change energy from one form to another and then use it to do work.

Forms of energy: Mechanical, thermal, chemical, nuclear, electrical, light, heat, and sound.

Potential Energy

Potential Energy (PE) is the stored energy in an object. This stored energy occurs due to its position or change in shape.

Examples of potential energy:

  • An archer draws an arrow with a bow (energy stored in the bent bow)
  • A ball on top of a slope
  • A book on a table
  • A mango fruit attached to its tree
  • A compressed or stretched spring
  • A swinging pendulum at the extreme ends
  • A man sitting on a wall

Gravitational Potential Energy

The potential energy due to elevated positions is called gravitational potential energy. The amount of gravitational potential energy possessed by an elevated object is equal to the work done against gravity in lifting it.

Formula: \(PE = mgh\)

Where:

  • \(PE\) = Potential energy (in Joules, J)
  • \(m\) = Mass (in kilograms, kg)
  • \(g\) = Acceleration due to gravity (9.8 ms⁻² ≈ 10 ms⁻²)
  • \(h\) = Height (in metres, m)

Worked Examples

Example 1: A body of mass 500kg was lifted through a vertical height of 20m. What is the P.E of the body? (Take \(g = 10m/s^2\))

\(PE = mgh = 500 \times 10 \times 20 = 100,000\) J

Example 2: What work is done when a mass of 200kg is lifted through a vertical height of 70cm? (Take \(g = 10m/s^2\))

\(h = 70cm = 0.7m\)

\(PE = mgh = 200 \times 10 \times 0.7 = 1,400\) J

Example 3: An object of mass 400g is raised vertically to a height of 40cm. If gravity is \(10m/s^2\), calculate the PE.

\(m = 400g = 0.4kg\), \(h = 40cm = 0.4m\)

\(PE = mgh = 0.4 \times 10 \times 0.4 = 1.6\) J

Example 4: A mango of mass 20kg and potential energy of 2000J is hanging on a tree. Calculate the height from the ground.

\(PE = mgh \Rightarrow 2000 = 20 \times 10 \times h\)

\(2000 = 200h \Rightarrow h = 10\) m

sessions.title: Kinetic Energy
indicators: Define kinetic energy
Give some examples of kinetic energy
Calculate questions involving kinetic energy
sessions.content:
Kinetic Energy

Kinetic Energy (KE) is the energy of a body in motion. It is the energy present in an object because of its motion.

Kinetic energy is gained whilst an object accelerates. Unless the speed of the object changes, the kinetic energy present is maintained.

Examples of kinetic energy:

  • An object rolling down a hill
  • A glass dropping to the floor
  • Running water
  • Movement of muscles while running or walking
  • A car moving
  • A falling ball
  • Rotation of blades in a blender
  • A rubber band flying through the air
  • Driving a bike

Formula for Kinetic Energy

The kinetic energy of an object depends on its mass and its speed.

Formula: \(KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2\)

Where:

  • \(KE\) = Kinetic energy (in Joules, J)
  • \(m\) = Mass (in kilograms, kg)
  • \(v\) = Velocity (in metres per second, m/s)

Worked Examples

Example 1: What is the KE of a body of mass 12kg moving with a velocity of 200m/s?

\(KE = \frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times (200)^2 = 6 \times 40,000 = 240,000\) J

Example 2: A body of mass 10kg moves with a speed of 5m/s. Calculate its kinetic energy.

\(KE = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times (5)^2 = 5 \times 25 = 125\) J

Example 3: How much work is done by a catapult to fire a 0.2g pebble at a velocity of 40m/s?

\(m = 0.2g = 0.0002kg\)

\(KE = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.0002 \times (40)^2 = 0.0001 \times 1600 = 0.16\) J

Example 4: If the kinetic energy of a moving body is 2000J and velocity is 10m/s, calculate the mass.

\(KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 \Rightarrow 2000 = \frac{1}{2} \times m \times 10^2\)

\(2000 = 50m \Rightarrow m = 40\) kg

Example 5: An athlete of mass 70kg runs a 100m race in 10s. Calculate the kinetic energy.

\(v = \frac{100}{10} = 10\) m/s

\(KE = \frac{1}{2} \times 70 \times (10)^2 = 35 \times 100 = 3,500\) J

sessions.title: Energy as Fuel
indicators: Enumerate some sources of energy that serve as fuel
Describe the types of fuel
sessions.content:
What is Fuel?

A fuel is any substance that can provide heat and produce energy when it is burned. The energy released is generally in the form of chemical energy or heat energy.

Examples of fuels: Coal, wood, oil, gas, methanol, gasoline, diesel, propane, natural gas, hydrogen.

Key point: The heat energy released by the reactions of fuels can be converted into mechanical energy via a heat engine.

Types of Fuels

1. On the basis of physical state:

Solid Fuels

  • Fuels that exist in their solid state at room temperature
  • Examples: Wood, Coal, Charcoal, Cow dung cake, Paraffin wax

Liquid Fuels

  • Fuels that exist solely in a liquid condition throughout their primary stage
  • Examples: Petroleum (Gasoline, Diesel, Kerosene), Coal tar, Biodiesel

Gaseous Fuels

  • Fuels that exist only in a gaseous state throughout their primary stage
  • Made up of hydrocarbons, carbon monoxide, hydrogen, or a combination
  • Examples: Natural gas, Butane, Propane

2. On the basis of occurrence:

Natural Fuels

  • Those that are in nature and that humans use to obtain energy
  • Examples: Firewood, Coal, Agricultural residuals, Petroleum, Wood

Artificial Fuels (Synthetic)

  • Those that humans obtain when a natural fuel is modified or intervened to increase energy output
  • Examples: Gasoline, Diesel, Kerosene, Butane gas

sessions.title: Energy Changes and Transformation
indicators: Mention some forms of energy
Give some examples of energy transformation
sessions.content:
Energy Transformation

Energy transformation is the change of energy from one form to another. When energy changes form, it is used to do work – such as making something move or making a living thing grow.

Law of Conservation of Energy: Energy can neither be created nor destroyed, but it can be transferred and transformed.

Examples of Energy Transformations

  • Chemical → Mechanical: Food is broken down and absorbed into muscles for movement
  • Chemical → Thermal: Food is converted to heat to keep the body warm
  • Electrical → Thermal: A toaster transforms electrical energy into heat
  • Electrical → Mechanical: A blender transforms electrical energy into motion
  • Nuclear → Electromagnetic: The sun transforms nuclear energy into ultraviolet, infrared, and gamma energy
  • Chemical → Mechanical + Electrical: Our bodies convert food into movement and electrical signals
  • Chemical → Thermal: A gas stove converts chemical energy from burning gas into heat
  • Potential → Kinetic: A ball dropped from a height changes potential to kinetic energy
  • Chemical → Light + Heat: A burning candle
  • Chemical → Mechanical → Sound + Heat: Rubbing palms together
  • Electrical → Light + Sound + Heat: A switched television
  • Chemical → Mechanical → Sound + Heat: A boy ringing a bell

Energy Transformation in a Torchlight

Chemical energy (dry cell) → Electrical energy (dry cell) → Light energy + Heat energy (bulb)

Energy Transformation of a Falling Mango

  • Before it falls: Potential energy
  • Midway: Both potential and kinetic energy
  • Just before hitting the ground: Kinetic energy
  • When it hits the ground: Sound + Heat energy

sessions.title: Meaning of Electronics
indicators: Define electronics
List some electronic components
Define electronic device
sessions.content:
What is Electronics?

Electronics is the branch of science that deals with the study of the flow and control of electrons (electricity) and their behaviour and effects in vacuums, gases, and semiconductors.

Electronics also deals with the devices that use such electrons.

Electronic Components

Every electronic equipment or system is made up of components or parts which are either active or passive.

Passive Components:

  • Capacitors – store electrical charge
  • Inductors – store energy in a magnetic field
  • Resistors – resist the flow of current
  • Diodes – allow current to flow in one direction only

Active Components:

  • Transistors – amplify or switch electronic signals
  • Thermistors – change resistance with temperature

Components are usually connected together to form circuits by soldering them to a printed circuit board (PCB).

Electronic Device

Electronic devices are components for controlling the flow of electrical currents for the purpose of information processing and system control.

Prominent examples: Transistors and diodes.

Electronic devices are mostly found in:

  • Television sets
  • Stereo systems
  • Computers
  • Digital cameras
  • Cell phones
  • iPods and portable media players

sessions.title: Identification of Household Electronic Appliances
indicators: Define an electronic appliance
Tell what a household appliance is
Name some household electronic appliances
Describe three household electronic appliances used in the living room and three in the kitchen
sessions.content:
What is an Appliance?

An appliance is any device or piece of equipment designed to perform a specific task. An appliance may or may not depend on electricity to operate.

For devices that depend on electricity, the electron flow may or may not be controlled by electronic components. If electron flow control is involved, the device is called an electronic appliance.

Household Electronic Appliances

Household electronic appliances (also known as domestic appliances) are electronic machines that help in household functions.

Examples of household electronic appliances:

  • Dishwashers
  • Dryers
  • Toasters
  • Food processors
  • Vacuum cleaners
  • Cookers
  • Refrigerators
  • Kettles
  • Microwaves
  • Air conditioners
  • Kitchen stoves
  • Water heaters
  • Washing machines
  • Freezers
  • Electric blankets
  • Radio

Appliances in the living room:

  • Television: Displays visual and audio entertainment
  • Radio/Stereo: Produces audio entertainment
  • Air conditioner: Controls room temperature

Appliances in the kitchen:

  • Refrigerator: Preserves food by keeping it cold
  • Microwave: Heats food quickly using microwave radiation
  • Blender: Mixes, crushes, and purées food

sessions.title: Uses of Household Electronic Appliances
indicators: Name specific household electronic appliances and describe their uses
Tell how well household electronic appliances should be handled and managed
sessions.content:
Uses of Electronic Appliances

Answering machine: Answers telephone calls and records messages

Generator: Produces a signal voltage or electrical power

Digital/Image Scanner: Generates a digital representation of an image for data input

Trackball: Positions the cursor and moves images on the computer screen

Amplifier: Takes a signal and increases its power

Smartphone: Makes calls, plays music, movies, games, chats, browses the internet, reads news, watches TV

Blender: Mixes, crushes, purées, or emulsifies food

Washing Machine: Washes clothes

Refrigerator: Keeps food fresh for longer periods

Freezer: Allows buying perishable food in bulk and keeping it fresh

Handling and Managing Electronic Appliances

  • Look out for the power consumption (rated in watts) and ensure it is in tune with the power source
  • Avoid overloading electrical power outlets or sockets – common cause of domestic fires
  • Avoid using extension cords or multi-outlet converters for appliances
  • Regularly check for signs of fraying, cracking, or other damaged conditions in electrical cords
  • Keep all used and unused cords tidy, secured, and away from children and pets
  • Unplug all appliances not in use to reduce potential risks and save power consumption (phantom drain)
  • Keep devices and outlets away from water to prevent shock
  • Appliances must be exposed to good ventilation to avoid overheating
  • Keep all exhaust fans clean – clogged fans can become a fire hazard
  • Read and observe the instructions in the user manual that comes with every appliance

sessions.title: First Aid and Basic Recovery Skills
indicators: Explain what First Aid is
Demonstrate some basic recovery skills
sessions.content:
Meaning of First Aid

First Aid is medical assistance provided to a person who has suffered a medical emergency. It is administered to preserve life, prevent the condition from worsening, or promote recovery.

Aims of First Aid:

  • To preserve life: Saving lives is the main aim
  • To prevent further harm: Keep the injured person stable until medical services arrive
  • Promote recovery: Taking steps to promote recovery (e.g., applying a bandage to a wound)

Basic Recovery Skills

How to Clean a Wound:

  • Clean the wound to prevent infection
  • Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water before touching the wound
  • Stop any bleeding by applying gentle pressure with a clean cloth, gauze, or paper towel
  • Rinse the wound with water and apply an antibiotic cream or ointment
  • Apply a bandage – helps reduce bleeding and acts as a barrier to germs

How to Stop Severe Bleeding:

  • Check the type of blood vessel – arterial bleeding is bright red and pulsates
  • Apply pressure on the wound with a sterile clean cloth
  • Raise the wounded limb over the heart to slow down the bleeding

How to Treat Shock:

  • Lay the victim on the back and raise their feet a foot higher than their head
  • Assure the victim is breathing – if not, administer CPR
  • Loosen any clothes and belts that seem uncomfortable
  • Cover the person with a blanket to keep them warm

Electric Shocks:

  • Switch off the current from the mains
  • Lay the patient flat on the floor in a recovery position
  • Keep the patient warm with a blanket
  • Administer artificial resuscitation if not breathing
  • Send the patient to the nearest hospital

Burns and Scalds:

  • Heat burns and scalds: Reduce pain by cooling with water – cover with dry sterile gauze and bandage
  • Chemical burns: Cut away contaminated clothing – wash with water for at least 20 minutes

sessions.title: Cardio-Pulmonary Resuscitation (CPR)
indicators: Know what cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) is
Demonstrate how cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) is performed in an emergency situation
sessions.content:
What is CPR?

Cardio-Pulmonary Resuscitation (CPR) is an emergency procedure, often employed after a cardiac arrest, in which cardiac massage, artificial respiration, and drugs are used to maintain the circulation of oxygenated blood to the brain.

CPR keeps blood and oxygen flowing when a person's heart and breathing have stopped. CPR performed within the first few minutes of the heart stopping can keep someone alive until medical help arrives.

Pulmonary/Lungs Resuscitation (Mouth to Mouth)

  • Place the casualty flat on the floor and kneel by the side of the upper body
  • Check the casualty's mouth with a finger to ensure there is no obstruction
  • Loosen all tight-fitting clothing on the person
  • Tilt the head backwards until the chin points straight up
  • Pinch the nostrils as you take a deep breath
  • Place your mouth over the casualty's mouth and blow air until the chest rises
  • Remove your mouth and watch the chest fall
  • Take in a fresh breath and repeat the procedure
  • Check the casualty's pulse to ensure the heart is beating

Cardiac Resuscitation (External Chest Compression)

  • With the casualty flat on the back, kneel next to the person's neck and shoulders
  • Place the heel of one hand over the centre of the person's breastbone
  • Place your other hand on top of your first hand
  • Keep your elbows straight and position your shoulders directly above your hands
  • Push straight down to compress the chest to about 4-5cm (for an average adult)
  • Relax the pressure by moving backwards
  • Perform 15 compressions at a rate of 80 times per minute
  • Go back to the casualty's head and give 2 mouth-to-mouth ventilations
  • Repeat with 15 chest compressions followed by 2 mouth-to-mouth ventilations
  • Check for a heartbeat after every 3 minutes

sessions.title: Health and Hygiene
indicators: Explain the meaning of health and hygiene
Describe the meaning of personal hygiene
Enumerate some personal hygiene practices
Describe some importance of health and hygiene
sessions.content:
Health and Hygiene

Health is described as a state of complete well-being both physically and mentally. A healthy person is one whose mind and body are completely fit.

Hygiene refers to practices and conditions that help uphold health and avert the spread of illnesses. It is habits or practices that ensure good health and a clean environment.

Personal Hygiene

Personal hygiene involves practices performed by an individual to care for one's bodily health and well-being through cleanliness.

Motivations for personal hygiene:

  • Reduction of personal illness
  • Healing from personal illness
  • Optimal health and sense of well-being
  • Social acceptance
  • Prevention of the spread of illness to others

Personal hygiene practices:

  • Showering or bathing regularly
  • Washing hands regularly (especially before handling food)
  • Washing scalp of hair
  • Keeping hair short or removing hair
  • Wearing clean clothing
  • Brushing teeth
  • Trimming fingernails and toenails
  • Covering one's mouth when coughing
  • Disposal of soiled tissues appropriately
  • Keeping toilets and food handling areas clean
  • Taking good care of eyes and ears
  • Keeping surroundings clean – throwing garbage in dustbins, not collecting water in drums

Importance of Health and Hygiene

  • Prevents the spread of diseases – washing hands regularly keeps illnesses at bay
  • Gives a good body image – influences self-respect, confidence, and motivation
  • Reduces health care costs – fewer occurrences of sicknesses
  • Increases social acceptance
  • Makes you feel energetic at work
  • Vital for children – optimum growth and development
  • Reduces risk of gum diseases and bad breath
  • Prevents dandruff and other scalp diseases
  • Sets an example for children
  • Professional acceptance – employers prefer well-groomed employees

sessions.title: Infections and Diseases
indicators: Explain the meaning of infections and diseases
Describe some infections and their diseases
Give examples of communicable and non-communicable diseases
sessions.content:
Infection

Infection is an attack and growth of microorganisms (pathogens) inside the body, causing harm to the body.

When a pathogen like a virus, bacteria, fungus, or parasite enters the body of a host and multiplies or replicates, causing harm to the host, it is called infection.

  • Infection can occur anywhere on the body, but is usually visible on the skin
  • Infection can be caused by a numerous variety of pathogens
  • Symptoms may be mild or serious

Disease

When the condition of the body gets altered from the normal and functional state to the abnormal and dysfunctional state, associated with signs and symptoms, it is called disease.

Communicable diseases:

  • Diseases that can be transferred from one organism to another
  • Transmitted through: contact with bodily fluids, insect bites, contaminated surfaces, water, food, or air
  • Caused by infecting agents like bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites
  • Examples: Malaria, Hepatitis C, Tuberculosis, Smallpox, Measles, Cholera, Influenza, HIV/AIDS, Ebola, STDs (Chlamydia, Gonorrhoea, Syphilis)

Non-communicable diseases:

  • Also called chronic diseases – progress slowly and last for a longer period
  • Main cause: genetic or environmental conditions or abnormalities since birth
  • Examples: Cardiovascular disease (heart attack, stroke), Cancer, Chronic respiratory disease (asthma), Diabetes mellitus
  • Genetic abnormalities: Down syndrome, Cystic fibrosis, Inborn errors of metabolism

sessions.title: Environmental Hygiene
indicators: Explain the meaning of environmental hygiene
Describe some activities for maintaining good environmental hygiene
sessions.content:
Environmental Hygiene

Environmental hygiene is a group of activities that aim to protect people from dangerous conditions. It covers all the practical prevention and control measures used to improve the basic environmental conditions affecting human health.

Environmental Conditions

  • Clean water supply
  • Human and animal waste disposal
  • Protection of food from contamination
  • Provision of healthy housing

Maintenance of Good Environmental Hygiene

1. Cleaning

  • A mechanical process (scrubbing) using detergent and water to remove food residues, dirt, debris, and grease
  • Essential in the prevention of infection

2. Disinfection

  • A process that uses chemicals (disinfectants) or heat to reduce the number of bacteria to a safe level
  • Examples: Household bleach, dishwashers

3. Clean Air

  • Air that has no harmful levels of pollutants (dirt, dust, airborne microbes, chemicals)
  • Essential to good health and a basic human need

4. Proper disposal of human and animal waste

5. Protection of food from contamination

6. Provision of healthy housing

Three major things to maintain environmental hygiene:

  • Clean our environment regularly
  • Disinfect the environment where necessary
  • Sustain clean air in the environment

sessions.title: Natural Resources and Their Uses
indicators: Define the meaning of natural resources
Identify the types of natural resources in the environment
Enumerate the uses of natural resources
sessions.content:
Natural Resources

Natural resources refer to the resources that exist on Earth independent of any human actions. They are materials available on the planet that can be used to keep people alive and meet their needs.

Examples: Oil, coal, natural gas, metals, stone, sand, air, sunlight, soil, water, animals, birds, fish, and vegetation.

Types of Natural Resources

1. Renewable (Exhaustible) Resources

  • Resources that are always available or can be replaced or recovered in a reasonable amount of time
  • Available in large amounts in nature
  • Examples: Water, Air, Sunlight, Wind

2. Non-renewable (Inexhaustible) Resources

  • Resources that cannot be replenished once they have been depleted
  • Available in nature but limited
  • Examples: Fossil fuels (oil, coal, natural gas), Minerals

Conservation of Natural Resources

  • Use less water – shorter showers, turn off taps when not in use
  • Turn off lights – use LED light bulbs
  • Walking, cycling, sharing vehicles – saves fuel
  • Use renewable energy – solar panels, wind energy, hydro energy
  • Use the three R's: Reduce, Reuse, Recycle
  • Compost – vegetable and fruit scraps enrich the soil
  • Avoid using plastics – they are non-biodegradable and increase environmental pollution

Uses of Natural Resources

  • Air: Oxygen, Wind energy
  • Water: Drinking, household, industries
  • Sunlight: Solar energy
  • Soil: Agriculture
  • Tree: Paper, timber, fuel, food products
  • Cotton: Clothing
  • Oil and Natural Gas: Fuel
  • Coal: Fuel
  • Iron Ore: Steel products
  • Gold, Platinum, Diamonds: Jewellery
  • Copper: Wires, coins, electrical products
  • Animals: Food, wool, milk

sessions.title: Effects of Human Activities on the Environment
indicators: Define the environment
Identify some effects of human activities on the environment
Describe the meaning of environmental degradation
Explain some of the effects of deforestation on the environment
sessions.content:
The Environment

The environment is everything that surrounds us. It encompasses all the natural, living, non-living, and artificial things. It also refers to the interaction of all living things with natural resources and other components of the environment.

Effects of Human Activities on the Environment

  • Overpopulation: The population of the world has reached more than seven billion today
  • Pollution: Causes global warming and climate change – caused by human activities
  • Farming: Food requirement has increased due to population growth
  • Meat Production and Poultry: Causes global warming
  • Manufacturing Industry: Produces food, paper, rubber, plastic, equipment, furniture
  • Erosion: Deforestation and littering of soil make it prone to erosion
  • Loss of Biodiversity: One of the major effects of human activities
  • Climate Change
  • Misuse of Natural Resources: Over-exploitation of natural resources

Environmental Degradation

Environmental degradation is a decline in the quality of our environment. This can be a result of:

  • Pollutants that spoil the air, water, or food supply
  • Over-extraction of resources
  • Destruction of habitats

Types of environmental degradation:

  • Destruction of natural habitats
  • Depletion of natural resources
  • Pollution of air, water, and soil
  • Extinction of wildlife

Impacts of environmental degradation:

  • Affects women's livelihoods – longer journeys to get primary necessities, greater exposure to risks of human trafficking, rape, and sexual violence
  • Increased poverty, famine, weather extremes, species loss, and disease

Deforestation

Deforestation is the clearing of forests to make way for human activities. It is a major threat to species' survival and a big conservation issue.

Causes of deforestation:

  • Farming
  • Grazing of livestock
  • Mining and drilling
  • Forestry practices
  • Wildfires
  • Urbanization

Effects of deforestation:

  • Habitat damage
  • Biodiversity loss
  • Aridity
  • Extinction
  • Changes to climatic conditions
  • Desertification
  • Displacement of populations

How to minimize deforestation:

  • Establishment of forest reserves
  • Bush burning and old farming practices should be discouraged
  • Reforestation: Planting new trees where trees have been cut down
  • Afforestation: The act of planting trees where there were none

sessions.title: Meaning of Science and Technology
indicators: Define science
Explain the meaning of technology
Describe some impacts of science and technology
sessions.content:
Definition of Science

Science is a continuing effort to discover and increase knowledge through research. Scientists make observations, record measurable data relating to their observations, and analyse the information at hand to construct theoretical explanations of the phenomenon involved.

The Scientific Method:

  • Observation: Making careful observations of natural phenomena
  • Hypothesis: Proposing a testable explanation
  • Experimentation: Conducting experiments to test the hypothesis under controlled conditions
  • Analysis: Analysing the results and drawing conclusions
  • Publication: Publishing work so other scientists can replicate and strengthen the results

Definition of Technology

Technology is the use of scientific knowledge for practical purposes in industry or in our everyday lives. Technology is an applied field concerned with developing, constructing, and applying ideas that result in apparatuses, gadgets, tools, machines, and techniques.

Key point: Whenever we use our knowledge gained in science to achieve some specific purpose, we are in the domain of technology.

Examples of technology:

  • The discovery of the wheel
  • Computers, tablets, and smartphones
  • Microwaves and MP3 players
  • Cars, aeroplanes, and locomotives
  • The light bulb and microchips
  • Space exploration technology

Impacts of Science and Technology

Positive Impacts:

  • New technologies: cars, aircraft, computers, cell phones, GPS, bio and genetic technologies
  • Improved quality of life in many areas

Negative Impacts:

  • Environmental destruction: Deforestation, soil erosion, destruction of land due to mining, manufacturing, highways, and housing
  • Extinction: Many species have become extinct
  • Overpopulation: Increased consumption and environmental destruction
  • Chemical, genetic, and nuclear pollution: Greenhouse effect, climatic changes, waste products
  • Weapons development: Technology has been used to improve weapons – killing has become psychologically easier

Quality of life should be measured by:

  • Health
  • Clean water and clean air
  • Healthy food
  • Beautiful nature
  • Creative working environment
  • Good art and culture
  • Love, good friends, and strong family

sessions.title: Contribution of Science and Technology to Food and Nutrition
indicators: Explain some of the scientific and technological processes in making food and nutrition
Give examples of processed food
sessions.content:
Food Processing

Food processing began about 2 million years ago when a distant ancestor discovered cooking, the original form of food processing. Humans first learned how to cook food, then how to transform, preserve, and store it safely.

Modern food science and technology integrate biology, chemistry, physics, engineering, materials science, microbiology, nutrition, toxicology, biotechnology, genomics, computer science, and many other disciplines to solve difficult problems.

Contributions of Science and Technology to Food

  • Food quality can be maintained or even improved
  • Food safety can be enhanced
  • Sensitive nutrients can be preserved
  • Vitamins and minerals can be added (fortification/enrichment)
  • Toxins and anti-nutrients (substances like phytate that limit bioavailability of nutrients) can be removed
  • Foods can be designed to optimize health and reduce the risk of disease
  • Waste and product loss can be reduced
  • Distribution around the world can be facilitated

Evolution of Food Processing Methods

  • Cooking → Canning
  • Sun drying → Mechanized and sanitized dehydration
  • Cool storage → Sophisticated refrigerators and freezers
  • Blast freezing and individual quick freezing – less detrimental to nutritional and sensory quality

'Hurdle Technology': Combining techniques to create conditions that bacteria cannot overcome (e.g., combining drying with chemical preservatives and packaging, or mild heat treatment followed by packaging and refrigerated storage).

Purpose of Food Processing

  • Preservation: Extend the shelf life of food
  • Safety: Remove health hazards associated with microbial pathogens (e.g., pasteurization of milk)
  • Quality: Ensure delivery of foods of the highest quality (taste, aroma, texture, colour, nutrient content)
  • Availability: Ensure consumer access to a wide variety of foods at any time
  • Sustainability: Ensure resources are used most efficiently
  • Convenience: Allow foods to be consumed after limited preparation
  • Health and Wellness: Enhance nutritional value through fortification and enrichment

sessions.title: Contribution of Science and Technology to Health
indicators: Identify the contribution of science and technology to health
List some gadgets used to address health issues
sessions.content:
Health

Health refers to a state of complete emotional and physical well-being. A healthful diet, exercise, screening for diseases, and coping strategies can all enhance a person's health.

Contributions of Science and Technology to Health

  • Improved treatment for numerous illnesses and serious health conditions
  • Medical research leading to new medications and methods of treatment
  • Advanced surgical instruments and techniques
  • CT scanners and other diagnostic equipment
  • Prosthetic limbs and other assistive devices
  • Wearable fitness technology: Smartwatches can track steps, monitor heart rhythms, ECG, blood pressure, temperature, and heart rate
  • Electronic health records: Storing, managing, and transmitting data becomes easy and quick
  • Virtual reality devices – help ease symptoms of depression and anxiety
  • Augmented reality – helps surgeons perform complex operations
  • Medical tourism: Patients can get in touch with specialists anywhere in the world for consultations and second opinions

Medical Gadgets and Devices

  • Surgical instruments
  • Oxygen delivery devices
  • Laboratory equipment
  • Fingertip Pulse Oximeter – measures oxygen saturation in the blood
  • Infrared Thermometer – measures temperature without contact
  • ECG machines – monitor heart activity
  • Blood pressure monitors
  • Self-adhesive biosensor patches – track temperature and heart rate

sessions.title: Contribution of Science and Technology to Transport
indicators: Explain the meaning of transport
Give some examples of the mode of transport
Describe how people move from one location to the other
sessions.content:
Transport

Transport is the movement of people, animals, and goods from one location to another. Transport is important since it enables trade between people, which in turn establishes civilizations.

Modes of transport:

  • Air: Aeroplanes, helicopters, spacecraft
  • Rail: Trains, trams
  • Road: Wagons, bicycles, motor vehicles (motorcycles, cars, trucks, buses)
  • Water: Ships, boats
  • Cable: Cable cars, ski lifts
  • Pipeline: Oil, gas, and water pipelines
  • Space: Spacecraft
  • Amphibious: Hovercraft, screw-propelled vehicles

Contributions of Science and Technology to Transport

  • Faster movement: Individuals can move rapidly from one place to another
  • On-Demand Ride Services: Uber, Bolt, Lyft – summon a vehicle with an app
  • Sensors: Communicate with GPS services to determine the best route
  • Accident Prevention: Sensors alert drivers to the position of other vehicles and prevent collisions
  • Safety: Sensors in the seat belt can track the driver's physiological indicators and determine whether the driver is fatigued or intoxicated
  • Drones and all infrastructures made with electronic parts make transport safer

Negative Impacts of Transport

  • Transport is a major use of energy and burns most of the world's petroleum
  • Creates air pollution – nitrous oxides and particulates
  • Significant contributor to global warming – emission of carbon dioxide
  • Traffic congestion and automobile-oriented urban sprawl – consume natural habitats and agricultural lands

Solution: Reducing transport emissions globally will have significant positive effects on Earth's air quality, acid rain, smog, and climate change.

sessions.title: Contribution of Science and Technology to Information
indicators: Explain information
Describe the contribution of science and technology to information
Mention some devices that help in passing on information
Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of modern modes of communication of information
sessions.content:
Information

Information is data that has been processed in such a way as to be meaningful to the person who receives it. It is anything that is communicated.

Examples of information: Timetables, shopping lists, report cards, printed documents, pay slips, receipts, reports.

Contributions of Science and Technology to Information

  • Internet and e-mail – changed the way people communicate
  • Satellite TV – people can have access to all programs at the same time
  • People can get information easily about their cultures, what they wear, eat, and how they behave

Modern Modes of Communication of Information (MMCI)

  • Smartphones: Text, talk, email, and video calls
  • Social media
  • Video conferencing
  • Skype

Advantages of MMCI

  • Reduces the importance of distance: Enhanced mail-order retailing, integrated transport companies
  • Saves time and money: Much cheaper and quicker to transfer information
  • Instant communication: Contact anyone around the world through emails and text messages instantly
  • Face-to-face communication: Skype, social media sites, video conferencing
  • New and exciting jobs: System analysts, computer programmers, web designers, hardware and software developers
  • Global village: The universe has become a global village due to information technology

Disadvantages of MMCI

  • Job unemployment: Through outsourcing, downsizing, and redundancies (e.g., factories replacing skilled personnel with robots)
  • Hijack by unscrupulous persons: Cybercrime and malicious programs that can cause severe damage
  • Fraud: Online banking fraud, mobile money fraudsters
  • Lack of personal contact: People communicate online without knowing whom they are really talking to
  • Cultural erosion: Some cultures have consumed others – teenagers in Western countries have influenced teenagers in other parts of the world

sessions.title: Industrialisation
indicators: Explain the meaning of industrialisation
Discuss the contribution of science and technology to industrialisation
sessions.content:
Industrialisation

Industrialisation is the process by which an economy is transformed from primarily agricultural to one based on the manufacturing of goods. It is characterized by economic growth, a more efficient division of labour, and the use of technological innovation to solve problems.

Key characteristics of industrialisation:

  • Economic growth
  • More efficient division of labour
  • Use of technological innovation
  • Mechanisation of production
  • Growth of cities
  • Modernisation

Contributions of Science and Technology to Industrialisation

  • Development of large and small-scale industries
  • Mechanisation of production – factories have brought down prices of commodities, improved quality, and maximized output
  • Growth of cities – people have started moving toward industrial areas in search of employment
  • Modernisation – adoption of modern ways of life and values (food habits, dress habits, speaking styles, tastes, preferences, ideas, values, and recreational activities)
  • Improved development of transport and communication – leading to national and international trade on a large scale

Negative Effects of Industrialisation

  • Unemployment: Machines provide employment opportunities for men but they also take away jobs through labour-saving devices – technological unemployment
  • Weapons development: Different weapons have been developed which have brought fear and nervousness to mankind
  • Greater technological advancement means more risk for mankind

sessions.title: Local Industries
indicators: Describe the process involved in the preparation of Kenkey
Explain the process involved in the preparation of bread
Outline the process involved in the preparation of alcohol
sessions.content:
Local Industries

There are several local industries within our communities. Most of these local industries still rely on simple equipment and skills in their operation. Most of their products are produced from raw materials cultivated within the community.

Typical local industries: Kenkey industries, Bread industries, Alcohol industries.

Kenkey Production

Ingredients: Corn (maize), water

Process:

  • Preparation of corn dough:
    • Soak the dry corn in water
    • After three days, drain and wash the corn
    • Grind it and add some water to make it into a dough
    • Keep it in a warm place to ferment for two to three days
  • Kenkey preparation:
    • Knead the fermented dough until thoroughly mixed
    • In a large pot, bring water to a boil. Slowly add one part of the fermented dough. Cook for about 10 minutes, stirring constantly. This half is called the 'aflata'.
    • Combine the 'aflata' with the remaining uncooked dough
    • Divide into serving-sized pieces
    • Wrap tightly in banana leaves, corn husks, or foil
    • Place the wrapped packets on a wire rack above water in a large pot
    • Steam for one to three hours
    • Serve at room temperature

Bread Production

Ingredients: Yeast, warm water, sugar, salt, unsalted butter, all-purpose flour

Process:

  • Dissolve yeast in warm water, let sit for 5 minutes
  • Add remaining water, sugar, salt, butter, and flour, stir to combine
  • Knead until a soft ball of dough forms (about 7-10 minutes)
  • Place dough in a greased bowl, cover, and let rise until doubled in size (45 minutes to 1 hour)
  • Turn dough out onto a floured surface and gently press to remove air pockets
  • Divide dough into two, roll into a tight cylinder
  • Place into greased loaf pans, cover, and let rise until doubled (30-45 minutes)
  • Bake at 400°F (200°C) for 30-35 minutes
  • Remove from oven, brush with melted butter
  • Cool completely before slicing

Alcohol (Akpeteshie) Production

Alcohol is a colourless, volatile, flammable liquid produced by the natural fermentation of sugars and is the intoxicating constituent of wine, beer, spirits, and other drinks. It is distilled from palm wine or sugarcane juice.

Process:

  • Palm wine is first tapped and fermented in a large barrel, sometimes with yeast
  • Fires are built under the barrels to bring the liquid to a boil
  • Vapour is passed through a copper pipe within cooling barrels, where it condenses and drips into sieved jars
  • The boiled juice then undergoes distillation
  • The resulting spirit is between 40% and 50% alcohol by volume

sessions.title: Meaning of Portfolio and Reflective Practice
indicators: Describe the meaning of the portfolio
Explain reflective practice
sessions.content:
Meaning of Portfolio

A portfolio is a purposeful collection of students' work that exhibits the student's efforts, progress, and achievements in one or more areas.

Key features of a portfolio:

  • Purposeful collection of students' work reflecting efforts and achievements
  • Evidence of self-reflection
  • Develops reflective thinking at various levels
  • Includes an 'envelope of the mind' for exploring feelings, values, beliefs, and dispositions

Two major categories of a portfolio:

  • Collection of evidence: Student-generated artefacts recording events and experiences
  • Reflection: Student teachers reflecting on what has been learned

Purpose of portfolios in teacher education:

  • Encouraging student teachers to document and describe their skills and competence
  • Provides much richer information than traditional assessment methods
  • Stimulates students to develop new understanding and appreciation of their experiences
  • Recognizes links between different aspects of experiences
  • Formulates insights to be tested in future actions

Reflective Practice

Reflective practice is learning through and from experience towards gaining new insights into self and practice.

Key points:

  • Reflection allows a pre-service teacher to articulate and investigate personal goals and evaluate progress
  • Encourages a positive attitude to learn throughout life
  • Reflection involves engagement in critical and analytical thinking
  • A reflective individual is likely inclined towards life-long learning
  • Reflective thinking is central to students' self-evaluation

Portfolio writing embodies reflective inquiry – each item in the portfolio is reflected upon.

Self-reflection focuses on:

  • Problem areas
  • What has already been learned
  • What still needs to be learned
  • Plans for how new learning might take place

Self-reflection fosters metacognition – a person's awareness of his or her thinking – and is the cornerstone of the reflective portfolio.

sessions.title: Writing Portfolio for Students' Reflective Journal (SRJ)
indicators: Explain the meaning of the reflective journal
Describe reasons to write a reflective journal
Discuss the use of a reflective journal
sessions.content:
Reflective Journal

A reflective journal is a way of thinking critically and analytically about your work in progress. It shows how different aspects of your work interconnect.

A reflective journal is a place to write down your daily reflection entries – something good or bad that happened that you can self-reflect and learn from.

Key points:

  • Can help you identify important learning events that happened in your life
  • Events include: relationship, career, and personal life
  • Provides a better understanding of your thought process
  • Reflective journal notes prepared by students reflect what they understand
  • This activity develops self-correcting and self-evaluating skills

Reasons to Write a Reflective Journal

  • To make sense of things that happened: Writing down details may give you a perspective you may not have otherwise considered
  • To speculate as to why something is the way it is: Your views can come from your own common sense, or from something you have heard at a lecture or read in a book
  • To align future actions with your reflected values and experiences: Continue to observe the subject to decide whether to stick to your original views or make changes
  • To get thoughts and ideas out of your head: Writing down thoughts can help relieve pressure or help resolve problems
  • To share your thoughts and ideas with others: Getting opinions from others can help you clarify your feelings for a deeper understanding of yourself

How to Write Reflectively – The Three W's

What (Description)

  • Recall an event and write it down descriptively
  • What happened? Who was involved?

So What? (Interpretation)

  • Reflect and interpret the event
  • What is the most important/interesting/relevant/useful aspect?
  • How can it be explained? How is it similar to/different from others?

What's Next? (Outcome)

  • Conclude what you can learn from the event
  • How can it be applied in the future?

Difference Between Portfolio and Reflective Journal

  • Portfolio: A collection of artefacts
  • Reflective Journal: A space where students critically reflect upon their own learning process

Prompt questions or guided reflections: The tutor/lecturer provides the area of the course that they would like the student to reflect upon.

sessions.title: Checklist for Professional Portfolio Assessment (Pre-Service)
indicators: Outline the key areas of a checklist for professional portfolio assessment
Mention some evidence of growth as an indicator of pre-service teaching competencies
State some reflective behaviour and strategies to manage the learning environment
sessions.content:
Checklist for Professional Portfolio Assessment

It is important to determine the criteria for assessing portfolios. Assessment criteria which have been used to determine the quality of the student's performance should be clear and easy to understand.

A. Professional Preparation

  • Candidate's Information: First name, last name, academic year, semester, programme
  • Statement of personal teaching philosophy: Discussion of your growth as a professional
  • Resume (Curriculum Vitae)
  • Child abuse and neglect recognition reporting certificate and first aid training
  • Letters of recommendation
  • Evaluations: Observation, Teacher Candidate Internship

B. Pre-service Teaching Competencies

Evidence of growth in knowledge, understanding, and disposition includes:

  • Learner-centred and/or differentiated instruction providing for special needs learners
  • Instructional and curricular adaptations for multicultural, ethnic, socio-economic, and linguistic diversity, and gender equity
  • Developmentally appropriate instruction
  • Standards-based instruction
  • Essential knowledge, skills, and processes in the content area
  • Ability to deliver instruction that uses inquiry, critical thinking, and problem-based learning
  • Engagement of learners in studies of the nature of the respective disciplines
  • Authentic learning experiences providing connections to human values and endeavours
  • Integration of technology and media resources for instruction, classroom organization, and pupils' learning
  • Various formal and informal methods of assessment and evaluation of learners to inform instruction and learning

C. Reflective Behaviour and Strategies to Manage the Learning Environment

  • Reflect on an individual learner's behaviour or a classroom behaviour encountered during student teaching that was challenging
  • Document how you handled the situation
  • What type of strategies did you use?
  • What was the outcome? (Successes and frustrations)
  • Did you believe the overall outcome of your strategies was successful? (Why or why not?)
  • What areas of growth did you experience from implementing this plan and working with this learner and/or group?
